entries.title: Lucy Caldwell: ‘Even your text messages fizz after reading James Joyce’
entries.summary: The author on the power of folktales, being inspired by Ulysses – and the book that every woman should readMy earliest reading memory The summer my middle sister was born, my mum and I would walk to the shops at Cherryvalley, in east Belfast, to do her messages, and she’d buy me the latest issue of Storyteller, illustrated tales from around the world. It came with a cassette tape of actors reading
entries.content:
My earliest reading memory

The summer my middle sister was born, my mum and I would walk to the shops at Cherryvalley, in east Belfast, to do her messages, and she’d buy me the latest issue of Storyteller, illustrated tales from around the world. It came with a cassette tape of actors reading the stories, a ding to tell you when to turn the page. My favourite was a Japanese folktale called The Goblin Rat. I was only just two, but I listened to it, entranced and terrified, until I could recite it by heart.

My favourite book growing up

Susan Cooper’s The Dark Is Rising series. I first read them aged 10 – I’ve read the title book every midwinter since, and read them last year with my own then-10-year-old son. They tell a haunting story of the age-old battle between the forces of dark and light, weaving in Arthurian legend, shot through with an adult melancholy that makes them as compelling now as then.

The book that made me want to be a writer

Jennifer Johnston’s How Many Miles to Babylon? We read it in English when I was 13 and our homework was to write an extra chapter. I spent every spare minute that week writing mine – the intoxicating feeling of tuning in to another world, until that world becomes more real than your own.

The book I came back to

I’ve always loved Dubliners by James Joyce, but first read Ulysses in paroxysms of undergraduate anxiety about not “getting” the references. Years later, I bought an edition with no footnotes, no endnotes, just the novel itself – it was, and continues to be, one of the most glorious reading experiences of my life. Even your text messages fizz after you’ve been reading Joyce.

The book I reread

Clarissa Pinkola Estés, Women Who Run With the Wolves. She’s a Jungian psychoanalyst and a cantadora, a keeper of the old tales, and this book is about the secret gifts and power of mythology, the paths to your inner psyche, reclaiming your wildness. I’ve read it in my 20s, 30s and 40s, and different stories have sung out to me each time. Every woman should read this book.

The book I could never read again

I love Rosamond Lehmann but I’ve never been able to get past the part in The Echoing Grove where two sisters try, ineptly and at agonising length, to bludgeon a rat to death.

The book I discovered later in life

As teenagers, we’d dare each other to leave the school library copy of Lady Chatterley’s Lover open on the “dirty bits” – where the spine was broken. But I didn’t actually read any DH Lawrence until I was in my 40s, and I’m so glad I came to that book later in life, as a wife, as a mother. It’s not the explicitness of the sex that’s shocking – it’s the ardency of the longing, the abandon of his rhythms. That would have been wasted on me before.

The book I am currently reading

I’ve just finished Helen Bain’s debut, The Daffodil Days, which I loved – an account of Sylvia Plath’s last year, told backwards, in an intricate series of stories by people momentarily close to her. It’s hard to know what to follow it up with, but I have high hopes for Courttia Newland’s The Art of Opposition, a book of essays “on hope, resilience and creative expression beyond the mainstream”, by a writer I know to be a formidably sharp and compassionate thinker.

My comfort read

It’s not that the stories themselves are comforting – far from it – but I reread Lorrie Moore’s Self-Help. There’s such pleasure in knowing that each story, like Philip Larkin’s definition of poetry, is a perfect verbal device to take you through its experience.

entries.title: Unsayable by Michael Cunningham review – The Hours author on the insecurities that powered his creative life
entries.summary: The American novelist and screenwriter’s memoir covers sex work, Virginia Woolf and Hollywood successAs an eight-year-old in comfortable, solid, suburban California, Michael Cunningham felt compelled to make art before he quite understood what it was, or could do: a painting of souls ascending to heaven, for example, in which paradise resembled the Griffith Park Observatory and the dead were all d
entries.content:
As an eight-year-old in comfortable, solid, suburban California, Michael Cunningham felt compelled to make art before he quite understood what it was, or could do: a painting of souls ascending to heaven, for example, in which paradise resembled the Griffith Park Observatory and the dead were all depicted in profile because their creator felt unequal to the challenge of full faces. There was also a cigar box covered in gold-sprayed macaroni and a clay representation of the animals on Noah’s Ark – anything, in fact, that would challenge the painting of a placid white chicken that hung in a gilt frame on his parents’ wall.

If Cunningham moved on fairly rapidly from the visual arts, either sparing or denying us the fruits of a more fully formed talent, it wasn’t because the impulse to create had withered. Rather, it was that the experience of making material art had functioned as a temporary outlet, being less painful, less embarrassing – both emotions he ascribes to his young, sensitive self – than attempting to capture the world in words.

Unsayable, an intriguing combination of memoir and writing guide, asks us to consider what causes those feelings of embarrassment and pain, and what powers them. Do they proceed, almost inevitably, from the gap between language and the vast, mysterious terrain it tries to describe? Cunningham’s writerly insecurity, from childhood to maturity, seems to live in that gap, and to feel it as an ever-present judgment on his endeavours, impossible to expunge. Success is no antidote and, he points out, might even contribute to a feeling of isolation from other writers, or a quickening fear of a return to obscurity.

Cunningham did become successful, and extremely so, when in 1998 he published The Hours, a triple-stranded, time-shifting riff on Virginia Woolf’s Mrs Dalloway; his celebrity increased four years later, when Stephen Daldry and David Hare released a film adaptation starring Nicole Kidman, Meryl Streep and Julianne Moore. Several novels and 25 years later, he was to return to a similar structure – compressed time and multiple interior monologues – in Day. “I don’t work on an epic scale”, he tells us in this book, “though my ambitions run to more epic proportions than I like to admit.” This, one might say, is the tension at the heart of modernism, perhaps Cunningham’s greatest stylistic influence: the attempt to render the grandeur of consciousness that also shows us its fragility, how easily it evaporates on examination.

Fragility and contingency recur throughout Unsayable. Cunningham might never have discovered Woolf unless he’d found himself secretly smoking at high school alongside a cool girl called Natalie, whom he desperately wanted to impress, despite his increasing suspicion that heterosexuality was not available to him. He’d overheard her mention Allen Ginsberg and asked what she was reading now. “It’s funny you should ask,” she replied. “I’m being just so completely transformed by Virginia Woolf.” Pretty soon, he was too.

There are plenty of observations that feel like familiar territory when it comes to writers on writing, most of them of the “fail again, fail better” variety. But where Cunningham is most engaging, and in a much more offbeat way, is in his repeated depictions of himself – mostly younger, but not always – as a man anxious to fit in, to lay claim to experiences that are far more recherché, more daring, than he really is. His flair for pace is no hindrance, and the chapter that begins, with no prior ground-laying, “During my senior year at Stanford, I started going to San Francisco on weekends to work as a rent boy” is a brilliant evocation of bravado, precarity and pathos.

So, too, are the episodes and people about whom he won’t write: his beloved sister, for instance, or – beyond a series of respectful vignettes – an elderly and gracious gay couple who befriended him when he briefly lived in Provincetown, who fed him delicious dinners and gasped delightedly in mock horror at his mildly exaggerated tales of sexual abandon. After their deaths, he wonders about the extent to which they had chosen their lives, or whether they had adapted to what, at the time, society would allow them to have. It’s a few pages, but so touching, so unsentimental, so enlarging a view of these two incidental men that you feel sure that Cunningham made the right choice, all those years ago, when he shelved the gold pasta cigar box.

entries.title: Air by Christian Kracht review – a metaphysical jigsaw puzzle
entries.summary: A portal opens from our modern world of data centres and interior design into a medieval fantasy realm, in this playfully surreal tale from the author of EurotrashA reclusive interior designer named Paul travels from his home in Orkney to the Norwegian coastal city of Stavanger for a most unusual commission. A data­centre housing a vast internet server is located by a fjord on the outskirts of the
entries.content:
A reclusive interior designer named Paul travels from his home in Orkney to the Norwegian coastal city of Stavanger for a most unusual commission. A data­centre housing a vast internet server is located by a fjord on the outskirts of the city, and a prestigious arts magazine is paying him a large sum of money to give it a makeover. They want him to paint the interiors white – “not just any white, but the perfect white, the singular white”. Shortly after he arrives, a freak weather event causes a power outage, and Paul goes missing; meanwhile the magazine’s jaded editor, Cohen, takes an overdose of barbiturates.

In alternate chapters a very different tale unfolds, set in a distant medieval world redolent of Norse mythology. A man, referred to only as “the stranger”, is nursed back to health by a nine-year-old girl who has shot him with an arrow after mistaking him for a deer. He is being pursued by a vengeful duke, and persuades the girl to join him on the run. Together they leave their plague-ridden town, traverse a barren wasteland and discover a subterranean stone city whose inhabitants live a communal existence, subsisting on seafood and kelp.

The reader experiences Christian Kracht’s new novel, Air, in a state of absorbed bewilderment: how on earth will these two narratives come together? There are obscure connections here and there – in a painting of Merlin and Lancelot that hangs in Paul’s home, and in passing references to neopaganism and Arthurian legend – and one startling anachronism, when the stranger shoots several of the duke’s henchmen with a modern handgun. After Paul’s disappearance, Cohen snoops through his bag and finds an instruction manual for 3D-printing a ceramic pistol. The mechanics of it all are tantalisingly opaque, but it seems as though the outage at the datacentre has produced a glitch that has jumbled up time, space and consciousness, opening a portal between a modern subjectivity and a medieval fantasy world.

During his first meeting with Paul, Cohen speculates that if a person’s thoughts can reside in physical space, as in a digital server, they might eventually transcend that person’s organic life. “What if memory could just persist? Would an entity in data storage have an ego, a self, or not?” Later on, the stranger becomes eerily conscious of himself as a character; he experiences an uncanny sense of deja vu, and has “the definite impression that the world was flattening, in a harmonious way, as though everything were becoming two-dimensional”.

This metaphysical jigsaw puzzle is the ethereal expression of a thoroughly mundane middle-aged ennui. Paul laments “this disgraceful world, with its lack of boundaries, Instagram, and the horrors of vernacular architecture”. He dreams of buying a property on a remote Inner Hebridean island populated only by feral horses. “No electricity, no heating, no phone reception. It was the perfect house.” Cohen is fed up with the modish aesthetics of Nordic chic (“cottagecore lichen restaurants, desaturated colours, that lonely European periphery”), and believes “the wars of the future would be waged out of boredom”.

Swiss author Kracht, who is in his late 50s, has built a reputation as a satirist of gen X affluence. The playful spirit that animated his previous novel, the International Booker-longlisted Eurotrash – in which a wealthy mother and son embark on a sentimental road trip through the Alps – gives way here to a dull, world-weary sardonicism. Paul and Cohen are archetypes of cosmopolitan privilege, their bland self-hatred conveyed in quietly damning cultural references – to Muji stationery, or Paul’s “90s normcore” wardrobe – and an emotionally inert prose, unfussily rendered in Daniel Bowles’s translation from the German. When Cohen overdoses, it is “not out of sadness … but because he just didn’t care”.

Amid such spiritual malaise, there is a bracing, wishful escapism in the sublime visions of natural beauty that punctuate the fantasy narrative: immense ice sheets and rock formations, migrating birds, the aurora. Cohen’s desire to redecorate the data­centre likewise has the feel of a therapeutic intervention: “Hopes, ideas, and memories amassed in such large quantities … painting this space white would ensure order, and clarity …” It’s a slightly ludicrous notion, but also just the kind of thing a dangerously overwrought mind might come up with – an attempt to declutter the psyche through a visual cleanse; a grandiose exercise in self-help.

The narrative artifice is somewhat flimsy, and the exposition oddly perfunctory at times. Yet the novel’s rough-hewn quality befits its evocation of a fuzzy, porous reality. Air is perhaps best understood as a parable – a warning, as it were, from the present. If consciousness, constituted in material infrastructure and therefore intrinsically vulnerable, can be corrupted or distorted like a radio signal, then the spectre of madness might never be too far away. Kracht has taken a familiar premise – brooding creative types, bored of their own cleverness – and made it suggestively strange.

entries.title: The Midnight Train by Matt Haig audiobook review – a cosy, feelgood journey
entries.summary: Actor James Norton brings a sense of gentle longing to this tale of an octogenarian looking back on the life he’s departedThe latest novel from the Reasons to Stay Alive author Matt Haig begins with an octogenarian man having a heart attack. Former business magnate Wilbur Budd is already feeling a bit peculiar when he receives a phone call from his ex-wife, Maggie, who he hasn’t heard from in year
entries.content:
The latest novel from the Reasons to Stay Alive author Matt Haig begins with an octogenarian man having a heart attack. Former business magnate Wilbur Budd is already feeling a bit peculiar when he receives a phone call from his ex-wife, Maggie, who he hasn’t heard from in years. “I wanted to hear your voice,” she tells him wistfully. Minutes after getting off the phone, Wilbur collapses on his front lawn and dies.

What sounds like the end of a story is, in fact, the beginning as Wilbur finds himself standing on a train platform with his mind “reverberating as if from some giant cosmic cymbal crash”. A train arrives and a woman disembarks and introduces herself as Agnes. She is, she says, the ghost of a bookseller who once ran a shop Wilbur visited as a child. She will be guiding him in a voyage through his past, pausing at formative moments including his brother’s premature death and Wilbur and Maggie’s honeymoon in Venice. “This is death,” Agnes tells him. “Where your life flashes before your eyes. This is how it happens. Fast and slow. Like life … ”

With shades of Dickens’s A Christmas Carol, The Midnight Train is a cosy feelgood tale, despite the ostensibly bleak themes of death and the afterlife. The narrator is the Happy Valley and McMafia actor James Norton who brings a rare softness and gentle longing to his performance. As Wilbur reflects on times gone by, he comes to realise the things in life he could have done better.

Available via Canongate, 8hr 36min

Further listening

You With the Sad Eyes

Christina Applegate, Headline, 8hr 27min

A candid memoir in which the actor best known as Kelly Bundy from Married … With Children reflects on her childhood raised by a heroin-addicted mother, becoming the family breadwinner as a child actor, and her recent diagnosis with multiple sclerosis. Read by the author.

Birnam Wood

Eleanor Catton, Audible Studios, 12hr 47min

Saskia Maarleveld reads this smartly plotted eco-thriller from the Booker prize-winning author of The Luminaries. It tells of a guerrilla gardening collective in New Zealand, named after the moving forest in Macbeth, who set their sights on a plot of land owned by an American billionaire.

entries.title: Raymond Briggs’s library goes on sale at his local Oxfam shop
entries.summary: Books belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his deathThe book collection of the creator of The Snowman and Fungus the Bogeyman has gone on sale at Oxfam’s Hove bookshop in East Sussex and online.More than 1,000 books from the collection of Raymond Briggs, the beloved British author and cartoonist, were donated by his estate after h
entries.content:
The book collection of the creator of The Snowman and Fungus the Bogeyman has gone on sale at Oxfam’s Hove bookshop in East Sussex and online.

More than 1,000 books from the collection of Raymond Briggs, the beloved British author and cartoonist, were donated by his estate after his death aged 88 in 2022.

Briggs’s personal library spans comics, illustration, novels and social history, including works by Peanuts creator Charles Schulz and Winnie-the-Pooh illustrator EH Shepard as well as a signed first edition of Graham Rawle’s Woman’s World, the 2005 book composed of fragments from women’s magazines.

Other illustrators featured in Briggs’s library include Shirley Hughes, Aubrey Beardsley, Edward Gorey and Posy Simmonds. There are about 30 comic annuals from the 50s, offering “a glimpse into the visual world that shaped Briggs’s style”, according to Oxfam.

“What’s fascinating is the range,” said Oxfam Hove bookshop volunteer Martin Hill. “You begin to see connections between the books Briggs created and the books and artists he chose to have around him.”

“There are great illustrators and cartoonists, but then you find books about ageing, bathrooms, domestic life, old annuals and wartime milkmen,” he added. “It is serious, funny, ordinary and sometimes rather dark – which feels very familiar to anyone who knows Briggs’s work.”

The collection includes pamphlets on nuclear disarmament, a subject explored in Briggs’s 1982 graphic novel When the Wind Blows. A 1974 book by Monty Python’s Terry Jones and Michael Palin, Bert Fegg’s Nasty Book for Boys and Girls, is also listed, along with a set of John Constable’s reproduced sketches and a first edition copy of Riddley Walker, the post-apocalyptic novel by Russell Hoban.

Born in Wimbledon in 1934, Briggs later spent more than 50 years living beneath the South Downs near Hove. The area inspired the 1978 picture book The Snowman, which features Brighton’s Royal Pavilion. Briggs taught illustration at Brighton School of Art from the early 60s to 1986.

He became known for picture books that subverted conventions of children’s storytelling, in the likes of 1977’s Fungus the Bogeyman and 1973’s Father Christmas, which imagines a cantankerous Santa Claus.

“I liked to find fantastical creatures and treat them realistically,” said Briggs in an interview with the Guardian in 2019, on the publication of his illustrated memoir Time for Lights Out. “I don’t see any difference between doing children’s and adult books. My attitude is exactly the same. I just do the book as I want it to be.”

The animated adaptation of The Snowman first hit screens in 1982, and has been broadcast almost every Christmas since.

After Briggs’s death, children’s author Michael Rosen said that “we have been lucky to have read and watched such great work from a great artist with a great heart”.

The Oxfam collection “offers a wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators”, said Hill. Prices for the books on the online store range from £5.99 to £400 for the first edition of Rawle’s Woman’s World.

entries.title: Children’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deleted
entries.summary: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social mediaBath children’s literature festival is facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.Authors said that their comm
entries.content:
Bath children’s literature festival is facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.

Authors said that their comments criticising the festival for its lack of representation under a post on its Instagram page were deleted, with the original post later removed and comments limited on other posts.

Several authors said they received emails from one of the founders, John McLay, on Monday evening asking them to delete their comments because they hadn’t “investigated the full line-up before hitting the keyboard”.

John McLay, a children’s literary scout, founded the festival 19 years ago alongside his wife Gill, an agent at Bath Literary Agency.

Gill McLay defended the decision to contact authors directly, saying the festival wanted a post with incorrect information, which claimed there were no writers of colour in the programme, to be removed.

The email cited seven authors of colour in the programme, one of whom is UK-Cypriot, as evidence that the complaints were inaccurate. The programme features almost 100 events in total, and bills itself as the UK’s largest children’s literature festival.

The festival also contacted one author’s agent to ask them to retract their comments.

“Maybe it wasn’t the right way to respond to it, and we have since apologised for that,” said Gill McLay. “It certainly wasn’t our intent to silence anybody at all. It was purely to just try and ensure that it was the right information.”

Children’s author Sophie Anderson, who received the email, told the Guardian: “To have someone so powerful in the industry sending these quite threatening and bullying emails is just horrible.”

Alexandra Sheppard, an author whose video about the situation has amassed more than 20,000 views, said that “the fear of being blacklisted for critiquing powerful organisations feels very real”. She is now organising an open letter to be sent to the festival.

In an Instagram statement, the festival said it is “very proud of this year’s line-up”, but had “listened to the comments made”. While it “chose authors and illustrators from the selection put forward by publishers”, it said it will “work harder to ensure we have greater representation”, adding that it has “already brought new people into the management of the festival to help us with this”.

Authors argue the issue reflects a broader pattern, with similar concerns raised about other events including the London children’s literature festival.

“In 2026, we need to do better,” said author Iqbal Hussain. “We are not living in Regency Bath, nor 1950s London.”

The row comes amid wider concerns about representation in children’s books. Recent Department for Education figures show that 38.7% of school-age pupils in England are from minority ethnic backgrounds, but the eighth Reflecting Realities survey, run by the Centre for Literacy in Primary Education, found that only 24% of books had main characters from racial minorities, down from 30% in 2022.

“I’ve seen countless conversations, panels, articles and social media posts about how publishing can better reflect the young readers it serves,” said Sheppard. “If an organisation like BCLF still lacks meaningful inclusion, the reason is simple: they don’t want it.”

Campaigners warn that limited representation can further alienate young readers who struggle to see their lives reflected in books.

Gill McLay told the Guardian that the festival is working to improve, citing steps such as introducing a steering committee. “All we’re asking of people today is that they give us the opportunity to do it better,” she added.

entries.title: Salman Rushdie attacker faces US terrorism trial
entries.summary: Hadi Matar, already serving 25 years for attempted murder, denies federal charges that could bring a life sentenceThe man who stabbed Salman Rushdie in the eye on stage in 2022 is standing trial for a second time – this time facing terrorism charges after being found guilty of attempted murder and sentenced to 25 years in prison last year.Hadi Matar’s federal trial begins in Buffalo, New York on W
entries.content:
The man who stabbed Salman Rushdie in the eye on stage in 2022 is standing trial for a second time – this time facing terrorism charges after being found guilty of attempted murder and sentenced to 25 years in prison last year.

Hadi Matar’s federal trial begins in Buffalo, New York on Wednesday, about 70 miles from the venue where Rushdie was due to speak about writers in political exile in August 2022, when he was stabbed more than a dozen times.

Matar, 28, was convicted last May of attempted murder and assault at the state level. He denies charges that include providing material support to terrorists and committing an act of transnational terrorism, and faces a possible life sentence if convicted on all counts.

Rushdie, 79, survived the attack but lost the use of one eye and suffered lasting nerve damage. The Booker prize-winning author is expected to testify again, having already given evidence at Matar’s state trial. The trial is expected to last approximately two weeks.

Central to the case is whether Matar was acting on the former ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini’s 1989 fatwa against Rushdie, calling for his death over his novel The Satanic Verses, which some Muslims regarded as blasphemous. Prosecutors have also argued that Matar was acting in service of Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militia in Lebanon.

Matar, a US-Lebanese dual citizen born in California, is reported by relatives to have grown withdrawn after visiting his father in Lebanon in 2018.

Investigators say Matar’s electronic devices and a personal journal showed detailed research into how an attack might be carried out. They also say it included a note weighing the case for acting swiftly given Rushdie’s age – that he could die “any day now”, “So NOW or NEVER!!!”. Photographs of Khomeini and his successor, Ali Khamenei, were reportedly found in Matar’s New Jersey bedroom.

Prosecutors also allege Matar produced and circulated propaganda videos in the months before the attack, incorporating a 2006 speech by the then leader of Hezbollah, Hassan Nasrallah, condemning cartoon depictions of the prophet. Court filings further claim Matar corresponded with an Iran-based contact in the months before the stabbing who said he knew “where to find Rushdie”. Matar also asked him whether the fatwa remained active.

Rushdie was born in India and moved to Britain aged 14. He won the Booker prize in 1981 for Midnight’s Children, before The Satanic Verses made him the target of threats. Iran has denied involvement in the 2022 attack, though the fatwa itself was never formally withdrawn. In 2024, Rushdie published Knife: Meditations After an Attempted Murder, a memoir about the attack.
